open_questions.notes: Son of Rev. John and Sarah (Worthington) Hooker of Northampton, MA. Rev. John descends through H00098 (John No. 2, b. 1695). Need EH entry number.
open_questions.action: Look up Rev. John Hooker of Northampton in EH index

military_service.notes: EH: 'though very young he had some service' — born 1759, so approximately age 15-18 during service

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Came to the Hinsdale homestead in 1789 through his marriage to Abigail Gay; worked the farm and made hats there for fifty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage carried Hookers north to Hinsdale, 1789
narrative_blocks.body: Born and raised at Northampton, he served briefly in the Revolution as a teenager and learned hatmaking. His 1789 marriage to Abigail Gay of Hinsdale brought him onto her father Bunker's homestead, establishing the Hooker family on what became their New Hampshire seat for four generations.

classification.lineage_note: H00526 Seth (gen 5) → H00264 Rev. John (gen 4) → H00098 John No.2 (gen 3) → H00030 John (gen 2) → H00007 Samuel (gen 1) → H00001 Thomas

open_questions.question: Father Rev. John Hooker of Northampton — EH entry number
open_questions.notes: Son of Rev. John and Sarah (Worthington) Hooker of Northampton, MA. Rev. John descends through H00098 (John No. 2, b. 1695). Need EH entry number.
open_questions.action: Look up Rev. John Hooker of Northampton in EH index

military_service.notes: EH: 'though very young he had some service' — born 1759, so approximately age 15-18 during service

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Came to the Hinsdale homestead in 1789 through his marriage to Abigail Gay; worked the farm and made hats there for fifty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage carried Hookers north to Hinsdale, 1789
narrative_blocks.body: Born and raised at Northampton, he served briefly in the Revolution as a teenager and learned hatmaking. His 1789 marriage to Abigail Gay of Hinsdale brought him onto her father Bunker's homestead, establishing the Hooker family on what became their New Hampshire seat for four generations.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born and raised on her father Rev. Bunker Gay's homestead; her 1789 marriage to Seth Hooker brought him onto the farm.
Inherited the Hinsdale homestead from his father Seth; died in the house where he had been born ninety-three years earlier.

career.notes: Learned the trade in early life; pursued carriage making while also farming the homestead.
Worked the homestead through old age; gave full attention to farming after abandoning the carriage trade.

narrative_blocks.header: His sons launched three California empires
narrative_blocks.body: Six of his eleven children left Hinsdale for the West. Charles Gay built the San Francisco hardware fortune, Henry Clay founded Arizona's first cattle ranch, and John Daggett funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson.

narrative_blocks.header: Ninety-three years in one Hinsdale farmhouse
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 1791 in the room overlooking the Gay homestead and dying in March 1885, he passed his entire life on one farm in Hinsdale. The Gazetteer of Cheshire County called him 'a hale and hearty old man.'

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed to California twice in his eighties
narrative_blocks.body: After eighty he made two trips out to visit his California sons, riding the railroads across a continent he had been born too early to see imagined. He returned each time to the Hinsdale homestead.

parents.father_research_notes: Simeon Daggett (1758-1819). Per WikiTree (cited in Sam's research). Likely descended from the Massachusetts colonial Daggett line (John Daggett of Watertown MA, immigrant ~1630s).
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Dean (1758-c.1819). The matching birth/death dates with Simeon (per WikiTree) are likely a data error in the WikiTree record — almost certainly Anna had different death date than Simeon. Maiden name Dean.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Henry Clay Hooker, Arizona cattle baron
narrative_blocks.body: She raised eleven children at Hinsdale on the Connecticut River. Her son Henry Clay Hooker founded the Sierra Bonita Ranch in Arizona Territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Daggetts married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1816 marriage to Henry Hooker came four years after Elisabeth Daggett, in a separate Hooker branch, married Edward Hooker. The two Daggett brides may share earlier Massachusetts ancestry.

X02247.siblings_research: Per WikiTree: 8 documented Daggett siblings: Simeon Jr (M, 1780-1854), Sarah M. (F, 1783-?), (unnamed son b.1784-?), Nathaniel (M, 1785-1826), Nancy (F, 1788-1854), Caleb C (M, 1792-1879), Silvey (F, 1792-1883 — possibly twin of Caleb), and Mary herself (1795-1864). Mary was the youngest of 8.

narrative_blocks.header: Amelia died young
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia died in 1817, in her first year.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born and raised on the homestead; married Henry M. Jones at Hinsdale in 1842 and remained in town.

sources.short: San Francisco city directories 1866-1905 (Hooker & Co. partner)
Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hooker brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay arrived in San Francisco in spring 1851 and partnered with younger brother John Delos Hooker at Hooker & Co., 117–119 California Street — the hardware fortune that later funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson. Older brother Henry Clay was simultaneously building Arizona's largest cattle empire.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined the Sons of the American Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay Hooker joined the Sons of the American Revolution, formally documenting his descent from a Revolutionary-era family. He died of pneumonia at age 83 on November 19, 1905, at his home at 967 Bush Street in San Francisco. Buried at Mountain View Cemetery in Oakland — the Bay Area's premier Victorian garden cemetery. (A Findagrave geocoding error lists the death location as 'San Francisco del Mar Municipality, Oaxaca, Mexico'; the actual location is San Francisco, California.)

narrative_blocks.header: A homestead chair followed him to San Francisco
narrative_blocks.body: He took a child's side chair from the Hinsdale homestead with him to San Francisco in 1851. The chair returned east after his 1905 death and now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society in Concord, provenanced back to his great-grandfather Rev. Bunker Gay.

cross_connections.display_label: Carried his great-grandfather Bunker's Gay surname as a middle name

burial.plot_notes: Mountain View Cemetery, Oakland, Alameda County CA (per Findagrave Memorial 255641260).

careers.employer: Hooker & Co. (with brother John Delos Hooker + Isaac H. Cory)
careers.notes: Came to San Francisco in spring 1851; built fortune in hardware import. Hooker & Co. imported hardware, iron, and related goods. Partnered with brother John Delos Hooker (H02709, who later founded the Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson Observatory).

Per Truman & Hooker partner biography (re. son Osgood): 'Charles G. Hooker, a prominent hardware merchant of California, who came to this State in 1852, and still resides in San Francisco.' Charles arrived CA 1852 — placed him in the second wave of Gold Rush migrants (1849-1852). Hardware merchandise business connects to the broader CA development boom serving miners + settlers.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up on the Hinsdale homestead before sailing to California in 1851; the family chair he took with him now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society.

narrative_blocks.header: Anson died young
narrative_blocks.body: Anson died in 1832 at the age of six. His parents gave the name to a brother born three years later.

bio.title: Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Formal portrait c.1907 (year of death) via Cloudinary

death.address: 870 West Adams Street, Los Angeles CA

sources.short: Willcox Cowboy Hall of Fame (1990 Posthumous Award)
National Cowboy & Western Heritage Museum (1959 Hall of Great Westerners)
Lynn R. Bailey, Henry Clay Hooker and the Sierra Bonita (book)

careers.notes: Hardware store; destroyed by fire Aug 10, 1865
Famous turkey drive 1865; pivoted to beef contracts 1867

careers.employer: U.S. Army (Apache War contracts) and Sierra Bonita Ranch
careers.notes: Founded Sierra Bonita Ranch 1872 in Sulphur Springs Valley AZ. Largest ranch in Arizona at peak (250,000 acres, 30,000 head). Honorary title Colonel.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded Sierra Bonita — Arizona's first cattle ranch

awards.award: National Historic Landmark designation (Sierra Bonita Ranch)
Cowboy Hall of Fame Posthumous Award

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hinsdale brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: Older brother Charles Gay built the San Francisco hardware fortune; younger brother John Daggett funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson — the instrument Hubble used to prove the expanding universe. Henry Clay went south to Arizona Territory and founded its largest cattle empire.

narrative_blocks.header: Sierra Bonita runs in the sixth generation today
narrative_blocks.body: After his 1907 death the ranch passed through son Edwin, grandson Harry, and on to sixth-generation operator Jesse Hooker Davis. The original Herefords from his 1880s importations still graze the 45,000-acre headquarters — Arizona's oldest ranch under continuous single-family ownership.

narrative_blocks.header: Hinsdale farmboy to Arizona cattle king
narrative_blocks.body: Born in 1828 on the family farm settled by his great-grandfather Rev. Bunker Gay, he left Hinsdale at twenty in 1848 and reached Arizona by 1867. He built a 250,000-acre empire two thousand miles from the Connecticut River Valley homestead where his life began.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost everything in a fire, then drove turkeys
narrative_blocks.body: After the 1865 Placerville fire destroyed his hardware store and home, he spent his last thousand dollars on five hundred live turkeys and drove them with two dogs over the Sierra Nevada to the Comstock Lode mining camps. He sold them for $1,750 — the capital that funded his Arizona move.

narrative_blocks.header: A cattle stampede chose Sierra Bonita's site
narrative_blocks.body: While moving ten thousand Texas Longhorns across Arizona in 1872, his herd stampeded and was found grazing in a verdant cienega beneath the Pinaleño Mountains. He homesteaded the spot, named it Sierra Bonita for the range, and built a U-shaped adobe fortress with twenty-inch walls and gun ports.

narrative_blocks.header: Sheltered Wyatt Earp after the Vendetta Ride
narrative_blocks.body: On March 27, 1882, the Earp posse fled to Sierra Bonita pursued by warrants for the killings of the Vendetta Ride. He fed them, gave fresh horses, refused to tell Sheriff Behan which way they had gone, and remained Earp's friend for the rest of his life.

cross_connections.display_label: Nephew Frederick joined him at Angelus-Rosedale, 1933
Nephew Cassius followed his Arizona ranching example

burial.plot_notes: Rosedale Cemetery, Los Angeles CA (no CEM entry yet — pending creation)

documents.notes: Standard biography by Lynn R. Bailey.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Founded the ranch in 1872; built and lived in its U-shaped adobe headquarters until his 1907 death.
Born and raised on the Hinsdale homestead until he left for New York at age 20 in 1848.

career.notes: Brief stint in NYC immediately after leaving Hinsdale at age 20.
Attached to the Indian Department, supplying goods on the Missouri frontier.
Mined the El Dorado County diggings before opening a hardware merchant operation at Placerville (Hangtown). His store and home burned in 1865.
Supplied beef to military posts and Indian agencies during the Apache Wars in partnership with Capt. Hugh Hinds.

Founded Arizona's first permanent American cattle ranch. Built U-shaped adobe fortress; expanded to 250,000 acres / 30,000 head. After 1891 drought reorganized as Sierra Bonita Land & Stock Co., capitalized $500,000.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born on the homestead; the Gazetteer of Cheshire County (1885) recorded her then residing on the homestead as a widow.
Born on the homestead; the Gazetteer recorded he 'owns and resides on the old homestead' as of 1885.

bio.extra_names: Born John Delos Hooker (per SF directories 1866 and Wikipedia); known professionally as John Daggett Hooker
bio.photo_notes: Photo via Cloudinary; sourced by Sam

death.cause: peritonitis (onset Sunday morning May 21; died Wednesday 8:15 PM May 24, 1911)
death.address: 325 West Adams Street, Los Angeles CA (LA Times obit said "425" — likely typo or renumbering; West Adams Heritage Association + Findagrave + Wikipedia all confirm 325)

sources.short: San Francisco city directories 1866-1885 (Hooker & Co. partner)
Linked institution: California Academy of Sciences (INST093)

notable.notable_blurb: Funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope, Mount Wilson

narrative_blocks.header: From Hinsdale farm to California iron baron
narrative_blocks.body: Educated at Holliston Academy and Williams College, he went to California in 1861 to join brother Charles Gay's San Francisco hardware firm. By December 1865 he had relocated to Los Angeles and built his fortune as Vice President of Baker Iron Works and President of Western Union Oil Company.

narrative_blocks.header: Funded one of history's most consequential telescopes
narrative_blocks.body: His $45,000 pledge in 1906 funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson — total gifts ultimately reached about $100,000. Edwin Hubble used the telescope in the 1920s to prove the universe is expanding, the foundational discovery of modern cosmology.

narrative_blocks.header: Charter member of Southern California Academy
narrative_blocks.body: Per Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy he was a charter member of the Southern California Academy of Sciences, founded 1891 in Los Angeles. He opened his private West Adams observatory to fellow Academy members.

narrative_blocks.header: John Muir wrote his late books here
narrative_blocks.body: Naturalist John Muir returned four times to write in the garret of the Hooker West Adams house in 1910-1911, doing much of his late-life work there. After Hooker's May 1911 death Muir wrote his own daughter Helen: 'I wonder if leaves feel lonely when they see their neighbors falling.'

narrative_blocks.header: His name passed to a great-nephew
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother Charles Gay's grandson — born in 1903 to Robert Gay Hooker and Elizabeth Lawton Shreve — was named John Rodman Hooker in his honor as the great-uncle who had funded the telescope. Tony Hooker, the modern descendant who has assembled much of the pedigree, traces his line through this John Rodman.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Henry Hooker Sr. of Hinsdale who made California fortunes — Charles Gay built the family hardware firm in San Francisco, Henry Clay founded Arizona's first cattle ranch in 1872, and John Daggett built the LA iron-and-oil empire that funded the telescope. Three brothers, three industries, three transformative Western enterprises.

cross_connections.display_label: Great-nephew was named in his honor after the telescope years.
Hosted Muir for late-life writing in his West Adams garret.
Funded his Mt. Wilson telescope, then estrangement before death.
built blast furnaces and founded the iron town of Anniston

careers.employer: Hooker & Co. (with brother Charles G. Hooker + Isaac H. Cory)
careers.notes: Brought into the family hardware-import firm in San Francisco. Built initial fortune in hardware and iron pipe trade.
Major LA iron and steel-pipe firm. Expanded family fortune significantly after 1886 LA move.
Diversified into California oil after iron-pipe success.

education.notes: Per EH 2709: preparatory education at Holliston Academy MA before Williams College
Per EH 2709: undergraduate at Williams College before going to California in 1861

memberships.notes: Per EH 2709: 'a member from its inception.' Founded 1891 in Los Angeles. NOTE: Wikipedia article on JDH says he 'founded the California Academy of Sciences' (founded 1853 in SF) — that is incorrect per EH primary source. He was a member from inception of the SOUTHERN California Academy of Sciences in LA, distinct from the older SF institution.
Wikipedia attributes 'founding' role; ambiguous given he didn't reach SF until 1861 (CAS founded 1853). May be conflation with Southern California Academy of Sciences.
Per LA Times May 25, 1911 obituary: "active member of the California Club"

Per LA Times May 25, 1911 obituary: "He was a member of the board of directors of the Southern California Academy of Sciences." This confirms the SCAS (LA, founded 1891) — NOT the California Academy of Sciences (SF, founded 1853) which Wikipedia incorrectly cited. Director-level membership suggests substantial active engagement, not just charter membership.

burial.plot_notes: Angelus-Rosedale Cemetery, Los Angeles CA. Family plot with wife Katharine Putnam (X02255), son Laurence Whitney (H04279), and daughter Marian Osgood (H04280).

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up on the Hinsdale homestead before sailing for California; later funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson Observatory.
Grew up on the Gay-Hooker homestead before her 1824 marriage to Elihu Stebbins Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Organized Hinsdale's first Sunday school
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1824 marriage to Elihu Stebbins Jr., she organized the first Sunday school in Hinsdale and appointed Lewis Taylor as its first superintendent. The Stebbins family had been Connecticut River Valley settlers since the 1650s.

parents.father_name: Elihu Stebbins Sr. (1762-1846, settled Hinsdale 1786, descendant of Rowland Stebbins of Ipswich 1634)

career.notes: Located on his father Elihu Sr.'s homestead and remained there his whole life.
Held the office of selectman; took an active interest in town affairs.
Repeatedly represented Hinsdale in the state legislature.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Bunker Gay's granddaughter on the homestead
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Gay Hooker, daughter of Seth Hooker and granddaughter of Rev. Bunker Gay, on 25 November 1824. Three children were born to them — John Mills, Sarah H., and Edward — all on the Stebbins homestead.

narrative_blocks.header: Selectman and repeated state legislator from Hinsdale
narrative_blocks.body: He held the office of selectman and repeatedly represented Hinsdale in the New Hampshire state legislature. The Gazetteer of Cheshire County noted he 'ever took an active interest in town affairs.'

narrative_blocks.header: Stebbins line traces to Ipswich, England, 1634
narrative_blocks.body: He descended from Rowland Stebbins, born 1594 in Ipswich, England, who sailed on the Francis in 1634 and went up the Connecticut River with William Pynchon to help found Springfield. His father Elihu Sr. brought the family to Hinsdale in 1786.

research_sources.page: Hinsdale chapter — Elihu Stebbins Jr. biographical sketch

education.notes: Dartmouth College class of 1848. Institution ID INST026 — verify against tree.

career.notes: Practiced law in Springfield, MA from after his 1848 Dartmouth graduation.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated Dartmouth College class of 1848 before reading law.

narrative_blocks.header: Dartmouth class of 1848 from Hinsdale homestead
narrative_blocks.body: He left the Stebbins homestead in Hinsdale for Dartmouth College and graduated in the class of 1848. He was the first of his Hooker-Gay descent line to pursue higher education beyond the local schools.

narrative_blocks.header: Elected Mayor of Springfield, Massachusetts, 1874
narrative_blocks.body: After settling in Springfield as a lawyer, he served as the city's mayor in 1874 — the most prominent municipal office held by any Hooker descendant of his generation in western Massachusetts.

career.notes: One of Hinsdale village's leading merchants per the Gazetteer of Cheshire County (1885).
Extensive real estate owner per the Gazetteer.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited his father's Hinsdale homestead
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited his father Elihu Jr.'s homestead and remained on it through his life. He resided in Hinsdale village, where he ran his merchant operation.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Hinsdale's leading merchants
narrative_blocks.body: The Gazetteer of Cheshire County (1885) described him as 'one of [Hinsdale's] leading merchants' and an extensive real estate owner. He combined the family homestead with a village commercial presence.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born and raised on the Gay-Hooker homestead before her 1820 marriage to William Hollis Taylor took her to a Taylor farm in Hinsdale.

career.notes: One of the church's leading laymen per EH.

institutions.institution_blurb: Appointed first superintendent of the Hinsdale Sunday school organized by his aunt Sarah Gay Hooker Stebbins.

narrative_blocks.header: First superintendent of Hinsdale's Sunday school
narrative_blocks.body: When his aunt Sarah Gay Hooker Stebbins organized Hinsdale's first Sunday school after her 1824 marriage, she appointed him as the school's first superintendent. The Gazetteer of Cheshire County (1885) called him 'the venerable Lewis Taylor.'

military_service.notes: Killed in battle at Antietam (Sharpsburg), 17 September 1862.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed at Antietam, fighting for the Confederacy
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Hinsdale, New Hampshire in 1840, he was killed at the Battle of Antietam on 17 September 1862 — age 21, fighting in the Confederate Army. His New England birth and Southern allegiance remain one of the family's least-explained passages.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried the Gay name into Confederate gray
narrative_blocks.body: His Gay middle name carried four generations from his great-great-grandfather Rev. Bunker Gay, the Hinsdale pastor settled 1763. The name reached its furthest geographic point on the bloodiest single day in American military history.

narrative_blocks.header: Charles died young
narrative_blocks.body: Charles died in 1851 at the age of eight.

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.
His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

parents.father_research_notes: EH says father was Deacon Thomas Hart — WRONG. Barbour Collection Farmington VR confirms father was Dea. Ebenezer Hart (X00119). Primary source wins.

sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

bio.notes: EH gives 'Mercy (Mary) Hart' — both names used interchangeably

burialCemetery.name: Hooker Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Named for the Hooker family. Land deeded to the Town of Irondequoit in 1840 by H00796 Alexander Allin Hooker, one of the town's founders and its first clerk. Family plot survived largely intact when much of the cemetery was accidentally bulldozed in the 1950s.

INST134.name: First Congregational Church of Hinsdale
INST134.notes: Organized 1763 with Rev. Bunker Gay as first settled pastor; he served 47 years. Original parish embraced Hinsdale plus Vernon on the Vermont side, until Vermont's statehood split the territory and weakened the church's membership and property. First wooden meetinghouse erected c. 1760; present structure built 1835-36, seats 550.
